data.title: Exxon Mobil Helium Supply Links Semiconductor Growth And Governance Questions
data.description:
• Exxon Mobil (NYSE:XOM) is gaining attention for its role as a major supplier of ultra high purity helium used in advanced semiconductor manufacturing.
  • The company is supplying six nines purity helium, a critical input for next generation chip fabrication processes.
  • Exxon Mobil is expanding helium liquefaction infrastructure, a move that could affect global chip supply chains and material availability.

For investors who know Exxon Mobil mainly for oil and gas, this helium exposure highlights the company’s connection to high tech manufacturing. Ultra high purity helium is essential for chip makers that are pushing into smaller nodes and more complex architectures, at a time when access to specialized materials is a key concern for the sector.

This helium build out may influence how you view NYSE:XOM in terms of diversification, supply chain relevance, and concentration risk in semiconductors. As helium volumes, contract structures, and infrastructure plans evolve, the importance of this business line to the overall equity narrative may become clearer.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Exxon Mobil by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Exxon Mobil.

See which insiders are buying and buying and selling Exxon Mobil following this latest news.

For current and prospective Exxon Mobil investors, the helium story sits alongside a separate strand of investor activity that is putting governance in the spotlight. On 8 April 2026, two shareholder groups filed proposals that go directly to how power is structured at the top of the company and how retail shareholders participate in votes. One calls for splitting the Chairman and CEO roles, which would affect how much authority is concentrated in Darren Woods’ position. The other focuses on Exxon's retail voting program and whether it gives too much weight to the board’s recommendations. Management has urged votes against both, so the upcoming 27 May 2026 annual meeting effectively becomes a test of how much influence governance focused investors currently have.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Exxon Mobil Narrative

  • The push for an independent chair and more neutral voting options aligns with existing concerns about regulatory and ESG scrutiny. Stronger board oversight can support long-term project execution in areas like Guyana and the Permian.
  • Management’s opposition to both proposals could challenge the narrative assumption that governance risks are manageable, especially as legal, regulatory and ESG pressures are already highlighted as a threat.
  • The narrative discusses capital returns and production growth, but does not fully address how repeated governance disputes over voting mechanics and board structure could affect Exxon's ability to pursue new low carbon ventures or adjust its portfolio quickly.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Exxon Mobil to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Repeated board versus shareholder clashes over issues like an independent chair and retail voting options could signal governance friction that some institutions may factor into their risk assessments.
  • ⚠️ If the retail voting program continues to lean toward board recommendations, smaller shareholders may feel their influence is limited, which can affect how engaged they remain with the stock during periods of stress.
  • 🎁 A more contested voting process can surface a wider range of views on topics like energy transition, capital allocation and Guyana exposure. This can give you more information for comparing Exxon to peers such as Chevron, Shell and BP.
  • 🎁 Clear disclosure of proposals and management’s stance allows investors to decide whether Exxon's current power structure matches their preferences on board oversight and long-term risk control.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, watch how much support each governance proposal receives at the 27 May 2026 annual meeting and whether Exxon adjusts its retail voting program in response. Pay attention to how large pension funds and long-term holders explain their votes, since that can hint at how they view Exxon's risk profile relative to other integrated oil majors. It is also worth tracking whether future proxy statements continue to attract similar proposals or if this year marks a turning point in board and shareholder engagement.

It was a workmanlike result, with revenues of JP¥61b coming in 3.8% ahead of expectations, and statutory earnings per share of JP¥520, in line with analyst appraisals. This is an important time for investors, as they can track a company's performance in its report, look at what experts are forecasting for next year, and see if there has been any change to expectations for the business. Following the latest results, Kasumigaseki CapitalLtd's three analysts are now forecasting revenues of JP¥156.5b in 2026. Before this earnings report, the analysts had been forecasting revenues of JP¥166.9b and earnings per share (EPS) of JP¥692 in 2026. So it looks like the analysts have become a bit less optimistic after the latest results announcement, with revenues expected to fall even as the company is supposed to maintain EPS.

It will come as no surprise then, that the consensus price target fell 5.2% to JP¥12,133following these changes. The most optimistic Kasumigaseki CapitalLtd analyst has a price target of JP¥13,400 per share, while the most pessimistic values it at JP¥10,000. Of course, another way to look at these forecasts is to place them into context against the industry itself. The analysts are definitely expecting Kasumigaseki CapitalLtd's growth to accelerate, with the forecast 60% annualised growth to the end of 2026 ranking favourably alongside historical growth of 45% per annum over the past five years. By contrast, our data suggests that other companies (with analyst coverage) in a similar industry are forecast to grow their revenue at 4.4% per year. It seems obvious that, while the growth outlook is brighter than the recent past, the analysts also expect Kasumigaseki CapitalLtd to grow faster than the wider industry.

The Bottom Line

The most obvious conclusion is that there's been no major change in the business' prospects in recent times, with the analysts holding their earnings forecasts steady, in line with previous estimates. Regrettably, they also downgraded their revenue estimates, but the latest forecasts still imply the business will grow faster than the wider industry. Even so, long term profitability is more important for the value creation process. data.title: Do Ebara's (TSE:6361) Earnings Warrant Your Attention?
data.description:
It's common for many investors, especially those who are inexperienced, to buy shares in companies with a good story even if these companies are loss-making. Sometimes these stories can cloud the minds of investors, leading them to invest with their emotions rather than on the merit of good company fundamentals. Loss-making companies are always racing against time to reach financial sustainability, so investors in these companies may be taking on more risk than they should.

In contrast to all that, many investors prefer to focus on companies like Ebara (TSE:6361), which has not only revenues, but also profits. Even if this company is fairly valued by the market, investors would agree that generating consistent profits will continue to provide Ebara with the means to add long-term value to shareholders.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

Ebara's Earnings Per Share Are Growing

The market is a voting machine in the short term, but a weighing machine in the long term, so you'd expect share price to follow earnings per share (EPS) outcomes eventually. That makes EPS growth an attractive quality for any company. Over the last three years, Ebara has grown EPS by 15% per year. That growth rate is fairly good, assuming the company can keep it up.

One way to double-check a company's growth is to look at how its revenue, and earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) margins are changing. While we note Ebara achieved similar EBIT margins to last year, revenue grew by a solid 11% to JP¥958b. That's progress.

The chart below shows how the company's bottom and top lines have progressed over time. Click on the chart to see the exact numbers.

View our latest analysis for Ebara

The trick, as an investor, is to find companies that are going to perform well in the future, not just in the past. While crystal balls don't exist, you can check our visualization of consensus analyst forecasts for Ebara's future EPS 100% free.

Are Ebara Insiders Aligned With All Shareholders?

Since Ebara has a market capitalisation of JP¥2.4t, we wouldn't expect insiders to hold a large percentage of shares. But thanks to their investment in the company, it's pleasing to see that there are still incentives to align their actions with the shareholders. Indeed, they hold JP¥4.7b worth of its stock. That shows significant buy-in, and may indicate conviction in the business strategy. Even though that's only about 0.2% of the company, it's enough money to indicate alignment between the leaders of the business and ordinary shareholders.

Does Ebara Deserve A Spot On Your Watchlist?

As previously touched on, Ebara is a growing business, which is encouraging. If that's not enough on its own, there is also the rather notable levels of insider ownership. The combination definitely favoured by investors so consider keeping the company on a watchlist. We don't want to rain on the parade too much, but we did also find 2 warning signs for Ebara (1 is concerning!) that you need to be mindful of.

Approach 1: Gates Industrial Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today’s value. It focuses on the cash the business is expected to generate for shareholders, not just current earnings.

For Gates Industrial, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $381.5 million. Analyst estimates are provided out to 2028, with Simply Wall St extrapolating further to build a 10 year path of free cash flow, ending with a projected $679.4 million in 2035.

When all those projected cash flows are discounted back, the DCF model produces an estimated intrinsic value of about $32.82 per share. Compared with the recent share price of around $24.91, this implies a discount of roughly 24.1%, meaning the shares are currently priced below this DCF estimate.

data.description:

Advertisement

Approach 1: Steel Dynamics Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes expected future cash flows, then discounts them back to today to estimate what the business might be worth right now. It is essentially a way of translating future cash generation into a single present value figure.

For Steel Dynamics, Simply Wall St applies a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model using cash flow projections expressed in US$. The latest twelve month free cash flow is reported as a loss of about $42.3 million, so the model leans heavily on expected future free cash flow rather than recent figures. Analyst and extrapolated estimates suggest free cash flow reaching about $3.3 billion in 2035, with intermediate projections such as $2.0 billion in 2026 and $2.6 billion in 2028, all discounted back to today using the DCF framework.

On this basis, the estimated intrinsic value comes out at roughly $310.17 per share, compared with the current share price of about $194.11. That implies a 37.4% discount, which indicates that Steel Dynamics is trading below this model-based estimate of fair value.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Steel Dynamics Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful guide because it links what you pay for the stock to the earnings the business is currently generating. The higher the expected earnings growth and the lower the perceived risk, the more investors are often willing to pay in terms of a higher P/E multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually supports a lower, more conservative P/E.

Steel Dynamics currently trades on a P/E of 23.7x. This sits above the peer group average of 20.2x and also above the broader Metals and Mining industry average of 22.9x. This suggests the market is applying a somewhat richer earnings multiple than these simple benchmarks. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Steel Dynamics is 27.4x, which is the P/E level implied by its model after factoring in elements such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market size and company specific risks.

This Fair Ratio can be more informative than a straight peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for those underlying differences rather than assuming all companies deserve the same multiple. Since the Fair Ratio of 27.4x is above the current P/E of 23.7x, this approach suggests the shares may be trading below that model based earnings multiple.

data.title: Has The Market Gone Too Far On Diageo (LSE:DGE) After Multi Year Share Price Weakness?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Diageo’s current share price reflects its long term potential, the key question is what you are actually paying for today.
  • The stock recently closed at £14.71, with returns of 2.3% over 7 days and 1.4% over 30 days, set against a year to date return of an 8.5% decline and a 1 year return of a 26.5% decline.
  • Over a 3 year period the share price return sits at a 55.9% decline and over 5 years at a 47.7% decline. This gives important context for anyone assessing whether sentiment has swung too far. These multi year returns frame the question of whether current pricing reflects temporary pessimism or a reset in expectations.
  • Simply Wall St’s valuation model gives Diageo a score of 4 out of 6. The rest of this article will break that down using different valuation approaches before finishing with a way to assess value that goes beyond any single metric.

Approach 1: Diageo Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting them back to today’s value. It focuses on the cash that could be available to shareholders, rather than accounting earnings.

For Diageo, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about US$2.58b. Analyst estimates and subsequent extrapolations by Simply Wall St suggest free cash flow projections rising into the low US$3b range from 2026, with a forecast of US$3.89b in 2030. Ten year projections combine analyst inputs for the earlier years with more mechanical growth estimates thereafter.

When those future cash flows are discounted back, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of US$28.27 per share. Compared with the recent share price of £14.71, this implies the stock is 48.0% undervalued based on this DCF framework.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Diageo Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is often a useful way to think about value because it links what you pay for each share to the earnings that business is currently generating. The level of P/E that might be considered normal or fair usually reflects what the market thinks about a company’s growth prospects and how risky those earnings are, with higher expected growth and lower perceived risk often supporting higher multiples.

Diageo’s current P/E is 18.4x, compared with an average of 16.8x for peers in its group and 17.1x for the Beverage industry. Simply Wall St also calculates a Fair Ratio of 23.5x for Diageo, which is the P/E that might be expected given factors such as its earnings profile, industry, profit margins, market value and risk characteristics. This Fair Ratio can be more informative than a straight comparison with peers or the industry because it adjusts for company specific traits rather than assuming all businesses deserve the same multiple. Compared with this Fair Ratio, Diageo’s actual 18.4x P/E sits below that level, which points to the shares trading on a lower multiple than this framework would imply.
